article_title: What I Actually Run in My Homelab – 2026 Self-Hosted Stack
core_thesis: DB Tech presents his 2026 self-hosted stack running on a ZimaCube, showcasing a comprehensive suite of open-source services that replace commercial subscriptions, enhance privacy, and provide practical tools for daily life, business, and development. The video emphasizes the value of self-hosting for data control, cost savings, and customization, while also including fun, non-essential projects.
detailed_summary: In this video, DB Tech walks through every service running on his ZimaCube homelab in 2026. The infrastructure is containerized (likely Docker) and includes a mix of essential, productivity, business, and fun applications. Key services include Koffan (a self-hosted bookmark manager), Obsidian LiveSync (for syncing notes), Home Assistant (smart home automation), Plex (media server), Vaultwarden (password manager), Cloudflared (secure tunneling), and Marreta (URL shortener). For content publishing, he runs Ghost, WordPress, YOURLS, Postiz, and LinkStack. Business tools include EspoCRM, Invoice Ninja, BookStack, Lenpaste (pastebin), FreshRSS (RSS reader), and n8n (automation). Development and fun services include Gitea (Git server), Gotify (push notifications), Speedtest Tracker, and a custom AI game. The video is sponsored by Faved, a self-hosted bookmark manager. DB Tech emphasizes that self-hosting gives him full control over his data and reduces reliance on third-party cloud services.

entities.name: DB Tech
entities.role: YouTuber and homelab enthusiast who presents his self-hosted stack

entities.name: ZimaCube
entities.role: The hardware platform running the homelab services

entities.name: Obsidian LiveSync
entities.role: Plugin for syncing Obsidian notes via self-hosted server

entities.name: Plex
entities.role: Media server for streaming personal media

entities.name: Cloudflared
entities.role: Cloudflare tunnel for secure remote access

key_data_points: DB Tech runs his homelab on a ZimaCube, a compact NAS/server device, demonstrating that a powerful homelab doesn't require enterprise hardware (🟢 relevant to homelabbing: shows a practical, low-power option for self-hosting).
Koffan is a self-hosted bookmark manager that stores bookmarks on your own server, replacing cloud-based services like Raindrop.io (🟢 relevant to privacy: keeps browsing data under your control).

Obsidian LiveSync allows real-time syncing of Obsidian notes via a self-hosted CouchDB or similar backend, enabling end-to-end encryption and full data ownership (🟢 relevant to productivity: secure, private note sync for students and professionals).
Home Assistant is used for smart home automation, integrating various IoT devices locally without cloud dependency (🟢 relevant to homelabbing: central hub for home automation with privacy focus).
Plex serves as a media server for streaming personal movie/TV collections, replacing services like Netflix for your own content (🟢 relevant to cost savings: eliminates streaming subscription fees).

Vaultwarden is a lightweight, self-hosted password manager compatible with Bitwarden clients, providing secure credential storage (🟢 relevant to cybersecurity: reduces risk of cloud password manager breaches).

Cloudflared creates a secure tunnel to expose services without opening ports, leveraging Cloudflare's network for DDoS protection and SSL (🟢 relevant to Red Teaming: understanding tunneling techniques for secure remote access).
Marreta is a self-hosted URL shortener, useful for creating custom short links under your own domain (🟢 relevant to homelabbing: simple service with branding control).

For content publishing, DB Tech runs Ghost (modern blogging), WordPress (traditional CMS), YOURLS (URL shortener), Postiz (social media scheduling), and LinkStack (link-in-bio page) – covering multiple aspects of online presence (🟢 relevant to students: can be used for portfolios or project blogs).

Business tools include EspoCRM (customer management), Invoice Ninja (invoicing), BookStack (documentation), Lenpaste (pastebin), FreshRSS (RSS reader), and n8n (automation) – a full suite for freelancers or small businesses (🟢 relevant to ambition: shows how to replace paid SaaS with self-hosted alternatives).

Development tools: Gitea (self-hosted Git server like GitHub), Gotify (push notifications for scripts/alerts), Speedtest Tracker (monitors internet speed over time), and a custom AI game (fun project) (🟢 relevant to programming: Gitea is essential for code versioning; Gotify can be integrated into CI/CD pipelines).
methodology_steps: 1. Choose a homelab server: DB Tech uses a ZimaCube, but any x86 or ARM device with Docker support works. Install a Linux OS (e.g., Ubuntu Server) and Docker/Docker Compose.
2. Set up a reverse proxy (e.g., Nginx Proxy Manager or Traefik) to route traffic to services and manage SSL certificates via Let's Encrypt.

3. Deploy services using Docker Compose files. For each service, create a docker-compose.yml with appropriate volumes, networks, and environment variables. Example for Vaultwarden: use image 'vaultwarden/server:latest', map port 80, set ADMIN_TOKEN.

4. For remote access, install Cloudflared (Cloudflare Tunnel) to expose services without opening firewall ports. Configure a tunnel in Cloudflare dashboard and run cloudflared as a Docker container with the tunnel token.
5. For note syncing, set up Obsidian LiveSync: deploy a CouchDB instance (or use a self-hosted backend) and install the LiveSync plugin in Obsidian, configuring the remote database URL.
6. For automation, deploy n8n with Docker, connecting it to various services (e.g., FreshRSS, email, webhooks) to create workflows that trigger actions based on events.
7. For development, deploy Gitea with a PostgreSQL database and configure it with SSH access for Git operations. Optionally, set up a CI runner (e.g., Woodpecker) for automated builds.
8. Monitor services using Gotify for push notifications (e.g., when a backup completes or a service goes down). Integrate Gotify with health checks or scripts.
9. For fun, deploy a simple AI game (e.g., a browser-based jumping game) using a lightweight web server or static site hosting within Docker.
conclusions: Self-hosting a comprehensive stack on a single device like the ZimaCube is feasible and can replace many paid subscriptions, from password management to media streaming. DB Tech's setup demonstrates that with Docker and proper configuration, one can achieve a high degree of data sovereignty and customization.

The inclusion of business tools (CRM, invoicing, documentation) shows that self-hosting is not just for tech enthusiasts but can support real-world professional needs, making it a viable option for freelancers and small businesses.

Security and privacy are central themes: using Vaultwarden, Cloudflared, and self-hosted alternatives to cloud services reduces exposure to data breaches and third-party surveillance. This aligns with Red Teaming principles of minimizing attack surface and controlling data flow.

The stack is modular and extensible; users can pick and choose services based on their needs. DB Tech's approach encourages experimentation and customization, which is ideal for ambitious homelabbers and students looking to learn.
personal_relevance_summary: This video is highly relevant to your homelabbing and DevOps interests, providing a concrete example of a production-like self-hosted stack with detailed service descriptions. As a programmer, you can directly apply tools like Gitea for version control, n8n for automation, and Gotify for notifications in your own projects. The cybersecurity angle (Vaultwarden, Cloudflared) offers practical insights into secure remote access and password management, which are valuable for Red Teaming (understanding secure configurations and potential misconfigurations). As a student, you can adopt Obsidian LiveSync for private note-taking and use the content publishing tools (Ghost, WordPress) for portfolios or blogs. The video also inspires you to build your own homelab, replacing costly subscriptions with self-hosted alternatives, saving money and gaining technical skills.
extra_advice: Consider replicating this stack in a virtual lab (e.g., Proxmox or VirtualBox) before committing to hardware. Start with core services (Vaultwarden, Gitea, n8n) and expand gradually. For Red Teaming practice, set up a vulnerable instance of one of these services (e.g., an outdated WordPress) and attempt to exploit it – this will deepen your understanding of both self-hosting and offensive security.
